description: Fix browser_execute_script so it returns the value of expression-body user code (including IIFEs) the way Chrome DevTools console and Node REPL do. Today the wrapper injects user code as statements inside an async function body, so expression values fall on the floor — `(function(){return 42})()` returns null. Change writeExecFile in platform/mcp-server/src/adapter-files.ts to evaluate user code expression-first (via indirect eval) and fall back to function-body/return semantics on SyntaxError. Update the tool description in platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/execute-script.ts to match the new contract. Add unit tests for the wrapper string shape and end-to-end E2E tests that execute real user code shapes (bare expression, IIFE, top-level return, multi-statement body, top-level await, expression returning a Promise, thrown errors) against a live Chromium tab. Resolves GitHub issue #79.

userStories.title: Rewrite writeExecFile wrapper with expression-first / statement fallback
userStories.description: Change the exec-script wrapper in writeExecFile to try the user's code first as an expression via indirect eval, and only fall back to function-body/return semantics on SyntaxError. This makes `(function(){return 42})()`, `42`, `document.title`, `await fetch(...).then(r=>r.json())`, `return 42`, and multi-statement bodies with explicit `return` all work uniformly — matching Chrome DevTools console and Node REPL. Also update the tool description and parameter description on browser_execute_script to match the new contract.
acceptanceCriteria: writeExecFile in platform/mcp-server/src/adapter-files.ts writes a wrapper that evaluates user code expression-first via indirect eval, then falls back to `new Function(body)()` / function-body semantics on SyntaxError only
The fallback is triggered only by SyntaxError — any other error (ReferenceError, TypeError) from the expression path propagates as the tool result error and is NOT retried as statements
Both the expression path and the statement path are wrapped in an async function so top-level `await` inside user code works in either shape
The resolved value is awaited and stored at `__ot[__resultKey] = { value }` (same shape as today); errors are stored at `__ot[__resultKey] = { error: <message> }`

The `__startedKey` sentinel is still set synchronously at the top of the IIFE before any try block, so the extension-side poller can distinguish 'IIFE hasn't executed yet' from 'sync code produced no result'
The wrapper still uses the namespaced key pattern (`__execResult_<uuid>`, `__execAsync_<uuid>`, `__execStarted_<uuid>`) so concurrent executions on the same tab do not collide

The browser_execute_script tool description in platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/execute-script.ts no longer contains the phrase 'use `return` for explicit values' and no longer contains the contradictory claim about 'the last expression value is returned' — replace with an accurate description matching DevTools/REPL semantics
The `code` parameter description in the same file no longer says 'The code is wrapped in a function body — use `return` to produce a result'
platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/execute-script.test.ts still passes unchanged

A new unit test file (or additions to platform/mcp-server/src/adapter-files.test.ts if one exists, otherwise create platform/mcp-server/src/adapter-files-exec-wrapper.test.ts) verifies the string shape of the wrapper emitted by writeExecFile: the wrapper contains an `eval(` call for the expression path, contains `new Function(` or equivalent for the statement fallback, sets __startedKey synchronously, and preserves the namespaced result keys
No references to the old 'code is wrapped in a function body' language remain in platform/mcp-server/src/
userStories.notes:
Files:
- platform/mcp-server/src/adapter-files.ts (rewrite the `wrapped = [...]` string in writeExecFile; header comment above the function also must be updated to reflect expression-first semantics)
- platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/execute-script.ts (tool description on lines 20-29 and `code` parameter description on lines 37-40)
- platform/mcp-server/src/adapter-files.test.ts OR platform/mcp-server/src/adapter-files-exec-wrapper.test.ts (add unit tests asserting the wrapper string shape)

Context:
GitHub issue #79 reports that `browser_execute_script` returns `null` when the script body is an IIFE like `(function(){return 42})()`, even though the IIFE itself returns a value. Root cause is in platform/mcp-server/src/adapter-files.ts:221-239 — user code is placed as a statement inside an async function body, so expression values fall on the floor and the async function resolves with undefined (normalized to null at extension-commands.ts:429).

Current wrapper (in writeExecFile, quoted exactly):
```
const wrapped = [
  '(function() {',
  '  var __ot = globalThis.__openTabs = globalThis.__openTabs || {};',
  `  var __resultKey = ${JSON.stringify(resultKey)};`,
  `  var __asyncKey = ${JSON.stringify(asyncKey)};`,
  `  var __startedKey = ${JSON.stringify(startedKey)};`,
  '  __ot[__startedKey] = true;',
  '  try {',
  '    (async function() {',
  code,
  '    })().then(',
  '      function(v) { __ot[__resultKey] = { value: v }; },',
  '      function(e) { __ot[__resultKey] = { error: e instanceof Error ? e.message : String(e) }; }',
  '    );',
  '  } catch (e) {',
  '    __ot[__resultKey] = { error: e instanceof Error ? e.message : String(e) };',
  '  }',
  '})();',
].join('\n');
```

Changes (target shape, conceptually — adapt to fit the existing var style):
```
const wrapped = [
  '(function() {',
  '  var __ot = globalThis.__openTabs = globalThis.__openTabs || {};',
  `  var __resultKey = ${JSON.stringify(resultKey)};`,
  `  var __asyncKey = ${JSON.stringify(asyncKey)};`,
  `  var __startedKey = ${JSON.stringify(startedKey)};`,
  '  __ot[__startedKey] = true;',
  `  var __src = ${JSON.stringify(code)};`,
  '  var __run = (async function() {',
  '    try {',
  '      // Expression-first: indirect (0, eval) runs in global scope so user code',
  '      // cannot accidentally capture wrapper-local identifiers. Parens force',
  '      // expression context so object literals and IIFEs work.',
  '      return await (0, eval)("(async () => (" + __src + "))()");',
  '    } catch (e) {',
  '      if (!(e instanceof SyntaxError)) throw e;',
  '      // Fallback: evaluate as a function body (statements with `return`).',
  '      return await (new Function("return (async () => { " + __src + " })();"))();',
  '    }',
  '  });',
  '  try {',
  '    __run().then(',
  '      function(v) { __ot[__resultKey] = { value: v }; },',
  '      function(e) { __ot[__resultKey] = { error: e instanceof Error ? e.message : String(e) }; }',
  '    );',
  '  } catch (e) {',
  '    __ot[__resultKey] = { error: e instanceof Error ? e.message : String(e) };',
  '  }',
  '})();',
].join('\n');
```

Key design notes for the worker:
- **Expression-first via indirect `(0, eval)`** — direct `eval` would run in local scope (capturing wrapper lexicals). Indirect `(0, eval)` evaluates in the global scope. The wrapper itself is injected via chrome.scripting.executeScript({ files, world: 'MAIN' }) which runs as extension-origin code and bypasses page CSP, so both `eval` and `new Function` work even on pages with strict CSP (existing strict-csp.e2e.ts proves this).
- **SyntaxError-only fallback** — any other error thrown by user code (ReferenceError, TypeError, user-thrown Error) must propagate to `{ error: ... }` without retrying. Retrying would double-execute side effects.
- **Why wrap the expression in `(async () => (EXPR))()`** — this turns any expression into an awaited promise resolution, so `await` at the top level inside user code works without requiring a `return`.
- **Why wrap the statement path in `(async () => { BODY })()`** — same reason; top-level `await` in statement bodies works.
- **Do NOT remove the namespaced key pattern** — concurrent executions rely on per-UUID keys. Reference adapter-files.ts:194-243 for current structure.
- **Do NOT change the `__startedKey` behavior** — extension-commands.ts:448 depends on this sentinel being set synchronously BEFORE the try/async work starts, so the poller can distinguish 'IIFE not executed' from 'result pending'.
- **JSON.stringify the user code once into `__src`** — cleaner than trying to embed the raw source twice; also safely escapes quotes/backticks.
- **Update the JSDoc** on writeExecFile (currently lines 184-193) to describe the new two-path semantics.

Tool description changes in platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/execute-script.ts:
Current lines 20-29:
```
  description:
    'Execute arbitrary JavaScript code in a browser tab and return the result. ' +
    "Code runs in the page's MAIN world with full access to the DOM, window, localStorage, and all page globals. " +
    'Bypasses page Content-Security-Policy restrictions. ' +
    'The last expression value is returned (use `return` for explicit values). ' +
    'Supports both synchronous and asynchronous code (Promises are awaited automatically). ' +
    'Examples: `return document.title`, `return localStorage.length`, `return document.querySelectorAll("div").length`. ' +
    'The return value must be JSON-serializable (strings, numbers, booleans, arrays, plain objects). ' +
    'DOM nodes, functions, and circular references cannot be returned. ' +
    'SECURITY: ...',
```
Replace the line `'The last expression value is returned (use `return` for explicit values). '` with something like:
```
  'The script is evaluated like the Chrome DevTools console or Node REPL: a single expression (including IIFEs and `await`) returns its value directly; multi-statement code uses function-body semantics with `return`. ' +
```
Also update the example list to include both shapes, e.g. `'Examples: `document.title`, `(function(){return 42})()`, `await fetch("/x").then(r=>r.json())`, `return document.querySelectorAll("div").length`. '`.

Current lines 37-40 (`code` parameter description):
```
      .describe(
        'JavaScript code to execute in the tab. The code is wrapped in a function body — ' +
          'use `return` to produce a result. Examples: `return document.title`, ' +
          '`return Array.from(document.querySelectorAll("script")).length`',
      ),
```
Replace with a description matching the DevTools/REPL contract, e.g.:
```
      .describe(
        'JavaScript to evaluate in the tab. Accepts a single expression (returned directly) ' +
          'or multi-statement code using `return`, like the DevTools console. ' +
          'Examples: `document.title`, `(function(){return 42})()`, `return document.querySelectorAll("script").length`.',
      ),
```

Unit tests: there is already platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/execute-script.test.ts (95 lines) — it tests the tool handler by mocking writeExecFile/dispatchToExtension/deleteExecFile. Those tests must still pass. For the wrapper string shape test, check whether adapter-files.test.ts exists (`ls platform/mcp-server/src/adapter-files.test.ts`). If it exists, add tests to it. If not, create platform/mcp-server/src/adapter-files-exec-wrapper.test.ts. The test should call writeExecFile with a fake state + known execId + code, then read the written file (via a spy on atomicWrite, or by stubbing atomicWrite and capturing the string argument) and assert the string contains the expected markers:
- contains `__ot[__startedKey] = true` before any `try` block
- contains `(0, eval)` for the expression path
- contains `new Function(` (or equivalent) for the statement fallback
- contains `e instanceof SyntaxError` guard on the fallback
- contains the three namespaced keys derived from execId

Constraints:
- Do NOT change extension-side code (platform/browser-extension/src/browser-commands/extension-commands.ts) — the result-reading contract (`__ot[__resultKey] = { value } | { error }`) stays identical. The bug is entirely in the wrapper template.
- Do NOT change the executeScript tool handler (platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/execute-script.ts) flow — only the description strings.
- Do NOT change the namespaced key scheme (`__execResult_<uuid>` etc.) — concurrent executions and the existing extension poller depend on it.
- Do NOT add a third 'mode' parameter to the tool surface — the whole point is the fix is invisible to callers.
- Do NOT remove the outer `(function() { ... })();` IIFE — file-based injection via chrome.scripting.executeScript requires a self-contained script.
- Do NOT introduce any dependency on page globals that might be shadowed by user scripts (e.g., don't rely on `window.eval`; use `(0, eval)` which resolves to the global eval).
- Do NOT change ADAPTER_WRITE_TIMEOUT_MS, EXEC_FILE_PREFIX, or any other constants.
- **Trap warning**: do NOT try to use `AsyncFunction` (`new (Object.getPrototypeOf(async function(){}).constructor)(...)`) as an alternative to the eval+Function split. It collapses both paths into one but does NOT capture an expression's value — you still need an explicit `return`, which is exactly the bug we're fixing. The two-path approach is the correct design.
- **Trap warning**: do NOT add a `try { return eval(X) } catch { return (function(){X})() }` pattern. The fallback must use `new Function(...)` so the body is parsed as a function body (which permits top-level `return`). A regular nested function would inherit closure scope and fail on `const`/`let` hoisting differences.
- **Trap warning**: the user-code string already comes through JSON.stringify in my design notes as `__src`. Do NOT also escape it further with `.replace(/`/g, ...)` or similar — JSON.stringify handles all necessary escaping for both single and double quotes and backticks (JSON forbids raw backticks only inside strings bounded by double quotes, which is what JSON.stringify emits).
- **Trap warning**: if you hit a Biome / TS error on `new Function(...)`, do NOT suppress it with a biome-ignore comment. The correct resolution is either typing it via `// biome-ignore lint/security/noGlobalEval: ...` — WAIT, this IS biome-ignore. Don't use biome-ignore. Instead, use a typed wrapper: `const Fn: new (body: string) => () => unknown = Function as unknown as new (body: string) => () => unknown;` or similar. Check the root biome.json and the existing codebase for an approved pattern for `eval`/`Function` usage (adapter-files.ts currently does NOT use either — but the wrapper string being generated here is a data string, not JS being evaluated by the Node process itself, so Biome's eval rules do NOT apply to the string content). This means the whole file is just string concatenation — Biome won't flag it.

Verification you can do locally while writing the PRD output:
- `cd platform/mcp-server && npm run build && npm run type-check && npm run test -- adapter-files` — unit tests pass
- Sample wrapper output for code `"(function(){return 42})()"` should contain both `(0, eval)` and `new Function(` and the namespaced keys.

userStories.title: Add E2E tests covering DevTools/REPL-like evaluation semantics
userStories.description: Add a new Playwright E2E test file that exercises browser_execute_script against a live Chromium tab with all the code shapes that a developer would reasonably expect to work, based on Chrome DevTools console and Node REPL semantics: bare expression, IIFE expression, object literal, top-level return, multi-statement body with return, top-level await, expression that evaluates to a Promise, intentionally thrown error, and undefined result. Pin the fixed behavior so it cannot regress.
acceptanceCriteria: A new file e2e/execute-script-semantics.e2e.ts exists and contains tests that use the `opentabsServer` fixture (or whatever fixture browser-chrome-apis.e2e.ts uses) to open a test page and call the browser_execute_script tool end-to-end
Test: bare expression `42` returns `{ value: 42 }`
Test: bare expression `'hello'` returns `{ value: 'hello' }`
Test: IIFE expression `(function(){return 42})()` returns `{ value: 42 }` (this is the exact repro from GitHub issue #79)
Test: arrow IIFE expression `(() => 'hi')()` returns `{ value: 'hi' }`
Test: object literal expression `({a: 1, b: [2, 3]})` returns an object with the correct structure
Test: top-level `return 42;` still returns `{ value: 42 }` (backwards compat with the documented workaround)
Test: multi-statement body with return `const x = 1; const y = 2; return x + y;` returns `{ value: 3 }`
Test: top-level await expression `await Promise.resolve(99)` returns `{ value: 99 }`
Test: top-level await inside multi-statement body `const r = await Promise.resolve(7); return r * 2;` returns `{ value: 14 }`
Test: expression evaluating to a Promise `Promise.resolve({ok: true})` returns `{ value: { ok: true } }`
Test: expression reading page DOM `document.title` returns the current page title as a string
Test: thrown error `throw new Error('boom')` returns `{ error: 'boom' }` (not a null value)
Test: expression that evaluates to `undefined` returns `{ value: null }` (undefined is normalized to null by the extension-side poller)
Test: ReferenceError from user code (e.g. `nonexistentVariable`) surfaces as `{ error: <message> }` — NOT retried as statements, NOT returning null silently

The E2E file uses the same test-server / fixture pattern as e2e/browser-chrome-apis.e2e.ts — specifically the `parseToolResult` helper to extract the structured content and the `value.value` shape assertion pattern
The new E2E file runs as part of `npm run test:e2e` with no flags needed
